topics.title: Patents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCEST206 - ENGINEERING ENTREPRENEURSHIP AND IPR
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Patents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Patent

A **Patent** is a statutory, time-bound, exclusive legal right granted by a sovereign government to an **inventor** (or the assignee of the inventor) in exchange for the public disclosure of a novel, non-obvious, and industrially useful invention. This right empowers the patent holder to legally **exclude** others from making, using, selling, offering for sale, or importing the patented invention for a limited territorial period, generally **20 years** from the date of filing, subject to the payment of renewal fees.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept)**
> A patent is an exclusive right granted for an invention, which is a product or a process that provides a new way of doing something, or offers a new technical solution to a problem. It is a territorial right — a patent granted in India (under the **Patents Act, 1970**) is enforceable only within the Republic of India.

The statutory body governing patents in India is the **Controller General of Patents, Designs & Trade Marks (CGPDTM)**, operating under the **Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT)**, Ministry of Commerce and Industry.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Land Deed" of Innovation

To understand a patent intuitively, consider this real-world analogy:

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Patent as a "Recipe Monopoly Card"** 🍲
> Imagine you invent a unique, never-before-tasted recipe for a magic soup that cures Monday-morning blues. You go to the City Hall (the **Patent Office**) and submit the exact recipe in a sealed envelope, describing the ingredients, the cooking process, and the exact measurements. The City Hall publishes your recipe in their public library (the **Patent Register** — fulfilling the *disclosure* obligation), and in return, gives you a **Patent Certificate** — an exclusive 20-year license that says: *"For 20 years, only you (or whoever you license) can commercially cook and sell this soup. If anyone else tries, they will be fined by the court."*
> After 20 years, the recipe becomes part of the **public domain** — anyone can use it freely, contributing to the collective knowledge of society.

The **land deed analogy** works equally well: A patent deed gives the "title" to a piece of intellectual real estate — it does not give ownership of the knowledge itself, but ownership over the *commercial exploitation* of that knowledge.

## 1.3 Core Distinguishing Terminology

For KTU examinations, students **must** precisely know the following one-liner distinctions:

| **Term** | **Precise Legal Meaning** |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Invention** | A new product or process involving an inventive step and capable of industrial application. |
| **Inventive Step** | A feature of an invention that involves a *technical advance* as compared to the existing knowledge (*prior art*) and has *economic significance*. |
| **Prior Art** | Everything made available to the public anywhere in the world before the filing/priority date of the patent application. |
| **Novelty** | The invention must not have been published, used, or known anywhere in the world before the date of filing. |
| **Non-Obviousness** | The invention must not be obvious to a *Person Skilled in the Art (PSA)* at the time of filing. |
| **Industrial Application** | The invention must be capable of being made or used in some kind of industry (the "utility" requirement). |
| **First-to-File** | India follows the **first-to-file** rule — rights go to whoever files the application first, not who invented first. |
| **PCT** | **Patent Cooperation Treaty** — an international filing system administered by WIPO, allowing a single "international" application to seek protection in multiple countries. |

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Patentability (Memory Anchor)

A patent is granted **only** when an invention simultaneously satisfies **ALL THREE** criteria. Failing any single pillar means rejection:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3 Pillars — N.U.N. Framework**
> 1. **N**ovelty (new, globally)
> 2. **U**seful (industrial application)
> 3. **N**on-Obvious (inventive step over prior art)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Patent Document (Specification)

A patent document is a highly structured legal-scientific instrument. According to the **Patents Act, 1970 (as amended in 2005)** and the **Patent Rules, 2003**, an Indian patent specification contains the following mandatory components:

1. **Title of the Invention** — Concise, descriptive, typically 1–15 words.
2. **Field of the Invention** — Broad technical domain (e.g., "Wireless Communication").
3. **Background of the Invention** — Discussion of *prior art* and the *problem* the invention solves.
4. **Summary of the Invention** — Brief overview of the technical solution.
5. **Brief Description of Drawings** — Explanation of any accompanying diagrams.
6. **Detailed Description of the Invention** — The complete, enabling disclosure (*sufficiency of disclosure*).
7. **Claims** — The **most legally critical section**; defines the *scope* of the patent's legal protection.
8. **Abstract** — A short technical summary (≤150 words).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Claims Matter Most** ⚖️
> The **Claims** section legally defines the boundaries of the inventor's monopoly. A competitor can freely practice everything *outside* the claims. During litigation, the court interprets the patent *literally* based on the claims. This is a **favourite KTU question** — *"What is the most important part of a patent and why?"*

## 2.2 The Three Types of Patent Applications (India)

The Indian Patents Act recognizes three distinct filing routes, each with different legal and economic implications:

- **Provisional Specification** — A placeholder filed to establish a *priority date*; must be followed by a **Complete Specification** within **12 months**, or the application is deemed abandoned.
- **Complete Specification** — The full, final patent application containing all technical and legal disclosures.
- **PCT International Application** — A single international filing under the **Patent Cooperation Treaty (WIPO-administered)** providing up to **30/31 months** of deferred national-phase entry.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Key Statutory Parameters

| **Parameter** | **Statutory Value / Rule** | **Governing Provision** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Maximum Term of Patent** | **20 years** from the date of filing | Section 53, Patents Act 1970 |
| **Provisional → Complete Deadline** | **12 months** | Rule 13, Patent Rules 2003 |
| **Working Statement (India)** | Must be filed every year in **Form 27** | Section 146(2) |
| **First-to-File Rule** | Priority is given to the *earliest filing date* | Section 11A, Patents Act 1970 |
| **PCT National Phase Entry (India)** | Within **31 months** from priority date | Rule 20, Patent Rules 2003 |
| **Grace Period for Disclosure** | **12 months** for disclosure at academic/scientific events | Section 31, Patents Act 1970 |
| **Examination Request Deadline** | **48 months** from priority date (or filing date) | Rule 24B(1) |
| **Opposition Period (Pre-grant)** | Any time *before* grant publication | Section 25(1) |
| **Opposition Period (Post-grant)** | Within **12 months** of grant publication | Section 25(2) |
| **Surcharge for Late Renewal** | Progressive: 6 months → 18 months → 30 months | Rule 80 |
| **Minimum Renewal Fee Periodicity** | Yearly (from year 3 onwards) | Section 142(1)(i) |
| **Territoriality** | Patent rights are **country-specific** | Article 4ter, Paris Convention |
| **Exclusive Marketing Right (EMR)** | Available during pre-grant opposition period | Section 24A (now historical) |
| **Compulsory License Grounds** | Non-working, high pricing, public health | Section 84, Patents Act 1970 |

## 2.4 Rights Conferred by a Patent

Upon grant, a patent confers on the patentee the **exclusive right** under **Section 48** of the Patents Act, 1970, to:

1. Prevent third parties from **making** the patented product.
2. Prevent third parties from **using** the patented product/process.
3. Prevent third parties from **selling** the patented product.
4. Prevent third parties from **offering for sale** the patented product.
5. Prevent third parties from **importing** the patented product for the purpose of sale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Subtlety — Right to USE vs. Right to EXCLUDE**
> A patent does **not** give the patentee the *positive right* to use the invention. It gives the *negative right* to *exclude* others. The patentee may still need separate licenses (e.g., regulatory approvals, or other patents) to actually commercialize the invention.

## 2.5 Non-Patentable Subject Matter in India (Section 3)

Under **Section 3** of the Patents Act, 1970 (as amended in 2005), the following are expressly **NOT patentable** in India — a frequent KTU question:

1. **Frivolous inventions** (Section 3(a)) — claims contrary to natural laws.
2. **Inventions contrary to public order or morality** (Section 3(b)).
3. **Discovery of any living thing or non-living substance occurring in nature** (Section 3(c)).
4. **Scientific principles or methods of mental/intellectual activity** (Section 3(k) — *business methods, algorithms*).
5. **Mere new use of a known substance** (Section 3(d) — controversial in *Novartis v. Union of India, 2013*).
6. **Plants and animals in whole or any part** (Section 3(j)).
7. **Computer programs per se** (Section 3(k) — judicial interpretation in *State Street*, *Rakesh Kumar*).
8. **Traditional knowledge** as such.
9. **Atomic energy-related inventions** (under the Atomic Energy Act, 1962).
10. **Aesthetic creations, films, and music** (protected by **Copyright**, not Patent).
11. **Schemes, rules, or methods of performing mental acts or playing games** (Section 3(15) — *State Street & St. Barts*).

## 2.6 Patent vs. Other Forms of IP — Critical Engineering Distinction

| **Criterion** | **Patent** | **Copyright** | **Trademark** | **Industrial Design** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Protects | Function/Invention | Expression | Brand Identity | Aesthetics/Shape |
| Term | 20 years | 60 years (after author's death) | 10 years (renewable) | 10 years (renewable) |
| Example | Engine, Algorithm (if technical) | Software code, Book, Song | Logo, Brand name | Shape of a bottle |
| Disclosure Required | Yes (full) | Not required | Not required | No (visual) |
| Needs Registration | Yes (for full protection) | Automatic (on creation) | Yes (for ®) | Yes (for full protection) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters for Engineers** 🛠️
> A new *algorithm* alone is not patentable in India, but a *computer-implemented invention* that produces a *technical effect* (e.g., faster image processing on hardware) **IS** patentable. Understanding this distinction is critical for software engineers and embedded systems developers — a key entrepreneurial concern in modern KTU evaluations.

## 2.7 The Concept of "Evergreening" — A Key Legal Battleground

> [!WARNING]
> **Evergreening Trap** 🚫
> **Evergreening** is the practice of obtaining fresh patents on minor modifications of an existing patented product (e.g., a new form, salt, or dosage) to extend the monopoly beyond the 20-year term. The **Novartis v. Union of India (2013)** Supreme Court judgment crystallized India's strict anti-evergreening stance under **Section 3(d)**, ruling that the *beta crystalline form of Imatinib Mesylate* was **not a new invention** because it did not demonstrate *significantly enhanced efficacy*. This is a **frequently-asked KTU question**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Process: The Patent Lifecycle in India

While patents do not involve mathematical "derivations," the procedural derivation of a granted patent is **highly structured** and is a **high-mark question** in KTU examinations. The complete lifecycle is derived below as a rigorous, ten-stage logical progression.

## 3.1 Stage-by-Stage Derivation of the Indian Patent Grant Process

> **Step 1 — Invention Conception & Documentation**
> The inventor conceives the invention. The earliest possible step is the creation of a **Laboratory Notebook** with dated, witnessed entries. The notebook is signed by a colleague who understood the invention, to provide evidence of the invention date in any future dispute.
> *(Justification: India is a first-to-file country, so the date of filing — not the date of invention — wins priority. However, documented evidence of invention is critical for assignment disputes and international priority claims.)*

> **Step 2 — Prior Art Search (Novelty Search)**
> The inventor (or attorney) conducts a comprehensive search across databases such as **Patentscope (WIPO)**, **INPASS (Indian Non-Patent Search System)**, **Google Patents**, and **Espacenet** to confirm that the invention is **genuinely novel** and **non-obvious** over global prior art.
> *(Justification: A failed novelty search can result in expensive filing fees and potential damages. Searches also help draft narrow, defensible claims.)*

> **Step 3 — Filing the Application (Provisional or Complete Specification)**
> Two parallel routes exist at this stage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Route A: Provisional Specification} &\longrightarrow \text{12-month deadline for Complete Specification} \\
\text{Route B: Complete Specification} &\longrightarrow \text{Direct examination-track entry}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The application is filed in **Form 1** (Application for Patent), **Form 2** (Specification), **Form 5** (Declaration of Inventorship), and **Form 26** (Power of Attorney, if filed through an agent). For foreign applicants, a foreign filing license is **no longer required** since the 2005 amendment.
> The **priority date** is established on this date.

> **Step 4 — Publication (18 Months Rule)**
> Under **Section 11A**, the application is published in the **Patent Office Journal** after **18 months** from the priority date. A request for **early publication** in **Form 9** is available and is often strategically used to begin building prior art against competitors.
> *(Effect of publication: The applicant gains **provisional rights** under Section 11A(4) — they can collect damages for any third-party infringement from the date of publication onward, but only after grant and only if the claims of the granted patent cover the infringing activity.)*

> **Step 5 — Request for Examination (RFE)**
> Under **Rule 24B(1)**, the applicant must file a **Request for Examination in Form 18** within **48 months** of the priority date (post-2016 amended timeline). Once filed, the application is queued for substantive examination.
> *(Strategic note: Filing the RFE earlier is recommended because the average first-examination pendency in India is typically several years. Delayed RFEs compress the effective 20-year term.)*

> **Step 6 — Substantive Examination by the Patent Office**
> A **Patent Examiner** examines the application for compliance with:
> 1. **Section 2(1)(j)** — Definition of invention.
> 2. **Section 2(1)(ja)** — Novelty.
> 3. **Section 2(1)(l)** — Inventive step (non-obviousness).
> 4. **Section 2(1)(ac)** — Industrial application.
> 5. **Section 3** — Non-patentable subject matter.
> 6. **Section 10** — Sufficiency of disclosure.
>
> The examiner issues a **First Examination Report (FER)** outlining objections, typically within 6–12 months of RFE filing.

> **Step 7 — Response to FER (Amendments & Arguments)**
> The applicant must respond to the FER within **6 months** (extendable up to **3 months** beyond that), addressing each objection through:
> 1. **Claim amendments** to narrow the scope.
> 2. **Technical arguments** distinguishing prior art.
> 3. **Affidavits or experimental data** demonstrating inventive step.
>
> Failure to respond results in the application being **deemed abandoned**.

> **Step 8 — Opposition Window (Pre-Grant)**
> At any time *before* the grant, any interested party may file a **Pre-Grant Opposition** under **Section 25(1)**, citing any of the grounds of patentability. The Controller then hears both sides.

> **Step 9 — Grant of Patent**
> If the Controller is satisfied on all grounds, the patent is granted, and the patent number is published in the **Patent Office Journal**. The **date of grant** is the date the patent is published. The patent becomes enforceable from this date.

> **Step 10 — Renewal, Annuities & Maintenance**
> Renewal fees are payable from **year 3** onward, with progressively increasing fees. Failure to pay the renewal fee within the prescribed window results in the patent **lapsing**. Restoration is possible within **18 months** of lapse via **Form 15** with a surcharge.

## 3.2 Consolidation: The Patent Grant Timeline

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Priority Date} &\longrightarrow \text{+18 months} \longrightarrow \text{Publication} \\
\text{Priority Date} &\longrightarrow \text{+48 months} \longrightarrow \text{RFE Deadline} \\
\text{Priority Date} &\longrightarrow \text{+20 years} \longrightarrow \text{Patent Term Expiry} \\
\text{Grant Date} &\longrightarrow \text{+12 months} \longrightarrow \text{Post-Grant Opposition Window}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Total Patent Grant Formula (Effective Term)

The **effective monopoly period** of a patent is mathematically expressed as:

$$
\boxed{
E_{\text{effective}} = T_{\text{statutory}} - T_{\text{prosecution}} = 20\,\text{years} - T_{\text{prosecution}}
}
$$

where $T_{\text{prosecution}}$ is the time between filing and grant. For a typical Indian patent with a 4–6 year prosecution period:

$$
\boxed{
E_{\text{effective}} \approx 14\,\text{to}\,16\,\text{years}
}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Effective Term Matters for Entrepreneurs** 💼
> An entrepreneur's **20-year statutory term** is not a guarantee — every year lost to prosecution, opposition, or appeal is a year of *lost monopoly revenue*. This is why companies use **PCT** and **Patent Prosecution Highway (PPH)** to accelerate examination in multiple jurisdictions.

## 3.4 PCT International Filing — The Global Patent Engine

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PCT Filing (WIPO, Geneva)} &\longrightarrow \text{+30/31 months} \longrightarrow \text{National Phase Entry} \\
&\longrightarrow \text{Independent grant in each designated country}
\end{aligned}
$$

PCT does **NOT** grant a "world patent." It provides:
1. **A single international application** for all PCT contracting states (now 150+ countries).
2. **An International Search Report (ISR)** — a global prior art report.
3. **An International Preliminary Examination (IPE)** — a non-binding opinion on patentability.
4. **A 30/31 month deferral** before national-phase filings are due, giving the applicant time to assess commercial viability.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Patent Lifecycle Flow — Indian Application (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Idea / Invention"] --> B["Prior Art Search"]
    B --> C{"Novel and Non-Obvious?"}
    C -- "No" --> D["Abort and Re-design"]
    C -- "Yes" --> E["Draft Specification"]
    E --> F{"File Provisional?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["Provisional Filing Form 1 and 2"]
    F -- "No" --> H["Complete Specification Filing"]
    G --> I["12 Months Deadline"]
    I --> H
    H --> J["Publication in 18 Months"]
    J --> K{"RFE Filed within 48 Months?"}
    K -- "No" --> L["Application Abandoned"]
    K -- "Yes" --> M["First Examination Report FER"]
    M --> N["Response to FER within 6 Months"]
    N --> O{"Controller Satisfied?"}
    O -- "No, Objections Persist" --> P["Hearing and Amendments"]
    P --> O
    O -- "No, No Resolution" --> Q["Refused"]
    O -- "Yes" --> R["Grant Published in Journal"]
    R --> S["12 Month Post Grant Opposition Window"]
    S --> T["Renewal Fees from Year 3"]
    T --> U["Valid for 20 Years from Priority Date"]
```

## 4.2 Patentability Criteria — Decision Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Invention Submitted for Examination"] --> B["Section 2 1 j Definition of Invention Met?"]
    B -- "No" --> C1["Rejected Not an Invention"]
    B -- "Yes" --> D["Section 3 Check Exclusions"]
    D --> E{"Excluded Subject Matter?"}
    E -- "Yes" --> C2["Rejected Section 3 Bar"]
    E -- "No" --> F["Section 2 1 ja Novelty Check"]
    F --> G{"Disclosed in Prior Art?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> C3["Rejected Lack of Novelty"]
    G -- "No" --> H["Section 2 1 l Inventive Step Check"]
    H --> I{"Obvious to a Person Skilled in the Art?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> C4["Rejected Obvious"]
    I -- "No" --> J["Section 2 1 ac Industrial Application Check"]
    J --> K{"Capable of Industrial Use?"}
    K -- "No" --> C5["Rejected Not Industrially Applicable"]
    K -- "Yes" --> L["Patent Granted"]
```

## 4.3 Patent Document — Component Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Title of Invention"] --> B["Patent Document Core"]
    C["Field of Invention"] --> B
    D["Background and Prior Art"] --> B
    E["Summary"] --> B
    F["Brief Description of Drawings"] --> B
    G["Detailed Description"] --> B
    H["Claims Most Legally Critical"] --> B
    I["Abstract Max 150 Words"] --> B
    B --> J["Specification Submission Form 2"]
    K["Form 1 Application"] --> J
    L["Form 5 Inventorship Declaration"] --> J
    M["Form 26 Power of Attorney"] --> J
    J --> N["Complete Patent Application Package"]
```

## 4.4 PCT vs. Direct National Filing — Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Single PCT Application at WIPO"] --> B["International Search Report ISR"]
    B --> C["International Publication in 18 Months"]
    C --> D["30 to 31 Months Deferral Window"]
    D --> E["National Phase Entry in Each Country"]
    E --> F1["Grant in Country A"]
    E --> F2["Grant in Country B"]
    E --> F3["Grant in Country N"]
    G["Direct National Filing Route 1"] --> F1
    H["Direct National Filing Route 2"] --> F2
    I["Direct National Filing Route 3"] --> F3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Define a **patent** and list the **three essential criteria** for an invention to be patentable under the Indian Patents Act, 1970.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A patent is an exclusive statutory right granted by the government to an inventor for a limited period (20 years) in exchange for public disclosure of a new, useful, and non-obvious invention. It confers the right to exclude others from commercially exploiting the invention. The **three essential criteria** for patentability under the Indian Patents Act, 1970 (Section 2(1)) are:
1. **Novelty** — The invention must be new, having no prior publication anywhere in the world before the filing date.
2. **Inventive Step (Non-Obviousness)** — The invention must not be obvious to a Person Skilled in the Art (PSA) at the time of filing.
3. **Industrial Application (Utility)** — The invention must be capable of being made or used in any kind of industry.
*(Award 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the three criteria.)*

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> Differentiate between a **provisional specification** and a **complete specification** in patent filing.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
| **Basis** | **Provisional Specification** | **Complete Specification** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Establishes priority date as a placeholder | Final, complete patent disclosure |
| **Detail Level** | Brief description of invention | Full technical and legal description with claims |
| **Deadline to File Complete** | Must be followed within **12 months** | N/A (it is the final filing) |
| **Legal Effect** | No grant; only priority date secured | Examined and prosecuted for grant |
| **Use Case** | Used when invention needs further R&D | Used when invention is fully developed |
*(Award 1 mark per key point, up to 3.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A — Optional 1

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for obtaining a patent in India**, from the date of filing to the date of grant, citing relevant sections and rules of the Patents Act, 1970. *(7 Marks)*
> **(b)** What is a **PCT application**? Explain its **benefits and limitations** in obtaining international patent protection. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Step-by-Step Procedure (7 Marks)

1. **Filing the Application** (1 Mark): The applicant files an application in **Form 1**, along with the **specification in Form 2**, **declaration of inventorship in Form 5**, and (if applicable) **Power of Attorney in Form 26**. Either a **provisional specification** (placeholder for 12 months) or a **complete specification** is filed. The **priority date** is established on this date.

2. **Publication of Application** (1 Mark): As per **Section 11A**, the application is published in the **Patent Office Journal** after **18 months** from the priority date. Early publication can be requested via **Form 9** (with prescribed fee). From this date, the applicant gains **provisional rights** to claim damages from infringers (subject to subsequent grant).

3. **Request for Examination (RFE)** (1 Mark): The applicant must file **Form 18** within **48 months** of the priority date (Rule 24B(1) post-amendment). Failure to do so results in the application being deemed abandoned.

4. **Substantive Examination by the Patent Office** (2 Marks): A Patent Examiner examines the application against the criteria of **Section 2(1)** (novelty, inventive step, industrial application), **Section 3** (non-patentable subject matter), and **Section 10** (sufficiency of disclosure). A **First Examination Report (FER)** is issued, listing objections.

5. **Response to FER and Hearing** (1 Mark): The applicant must respond to the FER within **6 months** (extendable by 3 months) through amendments, technical arguments, or experimental evidence. If objections persist, a **hearing** is scheduled before the Controller.

6. **Grant of Patent** (1 Mark): If the Controller is satisfied, the patent is **granted and published** in the Patent Office Journal. Post-grant opposition is open for **12 months** under **Section 25(2)**. The patent is then valid for **20 years** from the priority date, subject to renewal fees from year 3.

#### Part (b) — PCT Application (7 Marks)

The **Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT)** is an international treaty administered by the **World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO)** in Geneva, signed in 1970 and effective from 1978. It provides a unified procedure for filing patent applications in its **150+ contracting states**. A single **PCT international application** filed at a competent **Receiving Office** (such as the Indian Patent Office as ISA/RO) has the same effect as a regular national application in each contracting state.

**Benefits of PCT (4 Marks):**
1. **Single International Filing** — The applicant files **one application** instead of separate national applications in each country, drastically reducing initial costs, translation burdens, and administrative effort.
2. **18-Month International Publication** — The application is published internationally after 18 months, establishing global prior art and provisional rights.
3. **International Search Report (ISR)** — A high-quality prior art search is conducted by an **International Searching Authority (ISA)**, giving the applicant a clear picture of patentability before committing to expensive national-phase filings.
4. **30/31-Month Deferral** — The applicant has **30 months** (sometimes 31) from the priority date to enter national phases. This critical window allows time to assess commercial viability, secure funding, identify manufacturing/market partners, and defer substantial national-phase costs.

**Limitations of PCT (3 Marks):**
1. **No "World Patent"** — Despite its global nature, the PCT does **not** grant a single international patent. Grant remains the responsibility of each individual national/regional patent office, and additional substantive examination and translation costs are still incurred at the national phase.
2. **High Initial Costs** — The international filing fee, search fee, and translation costs can be significant, especially for small entrepreneurs.
3. **Dual National Phase Costs** — After the 30/31-month window, the applicant must pay national-phase fees, engage local patent attorneys in each country, and prosecute the application independently in each jurisdiction — a major cost burden.

---

### Question B — Optional 2

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** What are the **rights of a patentee** under the Indian Patents Act, 1970? Discuss the concept of **compulsory licensing** with reference to the *Natco v. Bayer (2012)* case. *(7 Marks)*
> **(b)** Explain the **concept of non-patentable subject matter** under **Section 3** of the Patents Act, 1970. Discuss the **Novartis v. Union of India (2013)** judgment and its impact on the pharma industry. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Rights of Patentee and Compulsory Licensing (7 Marks)

**Rights of a Patentee (4 Marks):**
Under **Section 48** of the Patents Act, 1970, a granted patent confers on the patentee the **exclusive right** to prevent third parties from:
1. **Making** the patented product.
2. **Using** the patented product or process.
3. **Selling** the patented product.
4. **Offering for sale** the patented product.
5. **Importing** the patented product for the purpose of sale.

These rights are **territorial** (limited to India) and **time-bound** (20 years from priority date). Importantly, the patentee has the right to **assign** the patent, **license** it (exclusively or non-exclusively), or **forgo** it. The patentee also has the right to **sue for infringement** in a District Court or High Court and seek remedies including damages, injunctions, and account of profits.

**Compulsory Licensing — Natco v. Bayer (2012) (3 Marks):**
A **Compulsory License (CL)** is a government-authorized permission to a third party to manufacture, sell, or import a patented product **without** the patentee's consent, in specific public-interest situations. **Section 84** of the Act lists the grounds, including: non-availability at a reasonably affordable price, non-working in India, and public health emergencies.

In the landmark **Natco Pharma v. Bayer Corporation (2012)** case, the **Controller General of Patents** granted India's first-ever compulsory license to **Natco Pharma** to manufacture the cancer drug **Sorafenib Tosylate (Nexavar)**, used to treat advanced kidney and liver cancer. The grounds were:
- The drug was **notably expensive** (~₹2.8 lakh/month for Bayer's version).
- Bayer had **not worked the patent in India** (no local manufacturing).
- Natco offered to sell a generic version at ~₹8,800/month (97% cheaper).
- Bayer had selectively imported and failed to meet public demand.

The CL was granted, with **Natco paying a 6% royalty** to Bayer. This judgment set a **powerful precedent** for affordable access to life-saving drugs, balancing IP rights with public health, and reinforced India's commitment to the **Doha Declaration on TRIPS and Public Health (2001)**.

#### Part (b) — Non-Patentable Subject Matter and Novartis Case (7 Marks)

**Non-Patentable Subject Matter under Section 3 (4 Marks):**
Section 3 of the Patents Act, 1970 (amended in 2005) explicitly bars the following from patentability:
1. **Frivolous inventions** or those contrary to natural laws (3(a)).
2. Inventions **contrary to public order, morality, or public health** (3(b)).
3. Mere **discoveries** of substances occurring in nature (3(c)).
4. Methods of **mental, intellectual, or business activity**, including mathematical methods (3(k)).
5. **Mere new use** of a known substance, unless it demonstrates **significantly enhanced efficacy** (3(d)).
6. **Plants and animals** in whole or part, including seeds and varieties (3(j)).
7. **Computer programs per se** (3(k)).
8. **Traditional knowledge** per se.
9. **Atomic energy** inventions.
10. **Aesthetic creations** (protected by Copyright).

**Novartis v. Union of India (2013) — The Anti-Evergreening Judgment (3 Marks):**
The landmark **Novartis AG v. Union of India (2013)** Supreme Court case centered on Novartis's attempt to patent the **beta crystalline form of Imatinib Mesylate**, the active ingredient in its blockbuster cancer drug **Gleevec**. The Court ruled **against Novartis**, holding that:
- The beta crystalline form was **not a new invention** under Section 2(1)(j).
- It **failed the "inventive step"** test under Section 2(1)(l) and Section 3(d).
- Under the **strict interpretation of Section 3(d)**, the new form did not demonstrate **"significantly enhanced efficacy"** over the known substance (the free base form of Imatinib).
- Mere improvements in **therapeutic efficacy** were not proven by scientific evidence (only animal model data was submitted, not human clinical data).

**Impact on the Pharma Industry (2 Marks):**
1. **Affordable Generics** — Indian generic manufacturers (e.g., Cipla, Natco, Sun Pharma) could freely continue producing affordable cancer drugs, supporting India's reputation as the **"pharmacy of the developing world."**
2. **Strict Patentability Standards** — Pharmaceutical multinational corporations must now demonstrate **clear, data-backed therapeutic superiority** for any new form of a known drug to secure a patent in India.
3. **Public Health vs. Patent Monopoly** — The judgment reinforced India's **public-health-first approach** to IP, in line with the Doha Declaration and TRIPS flexibilities.
4. **R&D Strategy Shift** — MNC pharma companies now focus on **genuinely novel molecules** rather than incremental modifications, changing the global pharmaceutical R&D landscape.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Easy Marks on Patent Questions** ⚠️
> 1. **Forgetting the 20-year term** — Several students write "15 years" or "10 years" — *instant 1-mark loss*. The correct statutory term under the Patents Act, 1970 (post-TRIPS amendment) is **20 years from the date of filing**.
> 2. **Confusing "right to use" with "right to exclude"** — Many answers state that a patent gives the patentee the "right to use" the invention. This is **wrong**. A patent gives the *negative right* to **exclude** others.
> 3. **Mixing Patent with Copyright terms** — A patent term is **20 years**; a copyright term in India is **60 years after the author's death**. Examiners deduct heavily for this confusion.
> 4. **Ignoring the First-to-File Rule** — When asked about patent disputes, students often discuss "who invented first." India follows the **first-to-file** rule, and global prior art is used.
> 5. **Omitting the Three Pillars (N.U.N.)** — Always list **Novelty, Utility, Non-Obviousness** explicitly. A vague answer like "the invention must be new and useful" loses the third pillar.
> 6. **Wrong forum for opposition** — Pre-grant opposition is filed **before** the grant; post-grant opposition is filed **within 12 months of grant publication** under **Section 25(2)**.
> 7. **Citing obsolete Sections** — Always cite the **2005-amended** version of the Patents Act, 1970 (especially Section 3(d)).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Patents** 📋
> - **Definition:** Patent = time-limited (20 years), territorial, exclusive *right to exclude* others from commercial exploitation of a publicly disclosed invention.
> - **Statutory Basis:** Patents Act, 1970 (as amended in 2005); Patent Rules, 2003.
> - **3 Pillars of Patentability (N.U.N.):** **N**ovelty, **U**seful (Industrial Application), **N**on-Obvious (Inventive Step).
> - **Rule:** India is a **First-to-File** country; priority is from the date of filing.
> - **Term:** **20 years** from the **priority/filing date** (not grant date).
> - **Publication:** **18 months** from priority date (Section 11A).
> - **RFE Deadline:** Within **48 months** of priority date (Rule 24B(1)).
> - **Opposition:** Pre-grant (anytime before grant) under Section 25(1); Post-grant (within 12 months of grant) under Section 25(2).
> - **Non-Patentable (Section 3):** Frivolous, immoral, natural discoveries, mental/business methods, computer programs *per se*, plants/animals, mere new use without enhanced efficacy.
> - **PCT:** Treaty administered by WIPO; provides **single international filing + 30/31-month national phase deferral**; **does NOT grant a world patent**.
> - **Evergreening:** Banned under **Section 3(d)** — see **Novartis v. UoI (2013)**.
> - **Compulsory License:** Available under **Section 84**; first issued in **Natco v. Bayer (2012)**.
> - **Working Statement:** Must be filed annually in **Form 27** under Section 146(2).
> - **Grace Period:** **12 months** for prior disclosure at academic/scientific events (Section 31).
> - **Most Legally Critical Section of a Patent:** The **Claims** — defines the legal scope of protection.
> - **Examination Time:** Average Indian patent prosecution is **4–6 years**; effective monopoly is therefore **~14–16 years**, not the full 20.
> - **Key Judicial Landmarks:** *Novartis v. UoI (2013)*, *Natco v. Bayer (2012)*, *Astrazeneca v. Intas (2020)*, *Ferid Allani v. UoI (2016)*.
> - **Entrepreneur's Key Takeaway:** The earlier and more rigorously a patent is filed, the longer the effective monopoly, and the greater the asset value of the IP for fundraising, licensing, and exit strategies (IPO/Acquisition).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trademarks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** UCEST206 - ENGINEERING ENTREPRENEURSHIP AND IPR > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Trademarks ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Trademark** is a distinctive sign, symbol, word, phrase, logo, design, or a combination thereof that identifies and distinguishes the goods or services of one enterprise from those of another. Under the **Trade Marks Act, 1999** (the governing statute in India), Section 2(1)(zb) defines a trademark as a mark capable of being represented graphically and which is capable of distinguishing the goods or services of one person from those of others, and may include the shape of goods, their packaging, and a combination of colours. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Trademarks form a critical sub-topic under Intellectual Property Rights (IPR). Students must master definitions, types, registration procedure, infringement, and remedies as the questions are frequently asked in **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a trademark as a **"face" of a product or company** in a crowded marketplace. Imagine you walk into a busy supermarket aisle with hundreds of similar products. How do you instantly spot your favourite? The swoosh of Nike, the golden arches of McDonald's, the bitten apple of Apple Inc. — these are not just logos; they are legally protected **identity stamps**. **Real-World Analogy:** Consider a student in a college campus. Each student has a unique name, a unique face, and a unique signature style. If another student copies the same name, ID card design, or dress code, there would be confusion in the campus. A trademark works exactly the same way — it prevents **consumer confusion** and protects the unique identity of a business. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition to Remember:** > A trademark = **Source Identifier** + **Legal Protection** + **Brand Equity Asset** ### 1.3 Essential Characteristics of a Valid Trademark For a mark to qualify as a registrable trademark, it must satisfy the following criteria: 1. **Distinctiveness** — Must be capable of distinguishing goods/services. 2. **Graphical Representation** — Must be representable on paper (in India, this is now relaxed to electronic representation as well). 3. **Not Deceptive** — Should not mislead the public about the nature, quality, or origin of goods. 4. **Not Prohibited** — Must not fall under the absolute or relative grounds of refusal under Sections 9 and 11 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999. 5. **Use or Intent to Use** — Either being used or proposed to be used in commerce. ### 1.4 Key Symbols Used with Trademarks | Symbol | Name | Legal Status | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **™** | Trademark | Unregistered | Claim of ownership; no legal registration required | | **℠** | Service Mark | Unregistered | Used for services instead of goods | | **®** | Registered | Registered | Legally registered with the Trademark Registry | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Trademarks (Structured Logic Breakdown) **Why classify?** Trademarks come in many forms, and KTU examiners frequently ask students to enumerate and explain types. Below is the comprehensive classification: #### A. Based on the Nature of the Mark - **Word Mark:** A trademark consisting of words, letters, or numerals (e.g., **Google**, **IBM**, **7-Eleven**). - **Device/Logo Mark:** A graphical design or symbol (e.g., the Apple logo, the Twitter bird). - **Composite Mark:** A combination of word and device (e.g., the Adidas wordmark with its three stripes). - **Sound Mark:** A distinctive sound (e.g., the Nokia tune, the MGM lion's roar). India recognized sound marks in 2017. - **Smell Mark:** An olfactory mark (rare; e.g., the smell of fresh-cut grass for tennis balls). - **Shape Mark:** The three-dimensional shape of the product or its packaging (e.g., the Coca-Cola bottle, Toblerone's triangular shape). - **Colour Mark:** A single colour or combination of colours (e.g., Cadbury's purple, Tiffany's blue box). #### B. Based on the Function Served - **Service Mark:** Identifies and distinguishes services rather than goods (e.g., the logo of an airline or a bank). - **Certification Mark:** Certifies the quality, origin, or standard of goods (e.g., **ISI mark** in India, **Woolmark** for pure wool). - **Collective Mark:** Used by a group or association of traders (e.g., a co-operative society's mark). - **Well-Known Mark:** A mark that has acquired widespread recognition among the public (e.g., **Coca-Cola**, **Nike**). India became compliant with the **Madrid Protocol** in 2013 and formally introduced the concept of "well-known marks" in 2017. ### 2.2 Grounds for Refusal of Trademark Registration **Why this matters:** KTU questions often test whether a student knows what *cannot* be trademarked. #### Absolute Grounds (Section 9 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999): - Marks devoid of distinctive character. - Marks consisting exclusively of marks which have become customary in trade. - Marks that are descriptive (generic terms). - Marks primarily consisting of surnames or geographical indications. - Marks that would deceive or cause confusion. - Marks contrary to public order or morality. - Marks containing the flag, emblem, or name of any State or international organization without authorization. #### Relative Grounds (Section 11 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999): - Marks identical to an earlier trademark for identical goods/services. - Marks identical or similar to an earlier trademark for identical or similar goods/services, creating a likelihood of confusion. - Use of an earlier trademark with reputation in India, causing unfair advantage or detriment. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield KTU Reference Table for Trademarks** | Parameter | Specification | Governing Provision | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Governing Law (India)** | Trade Marks Act, **1999** | Section 1, Trade Marks Act, 1999 | | **Definition of Trademark** | Section 2(1)(zb) | Statutory Definition | | **Absolute Grounds of Refusal** | Section 9 | Non-distinctive, descriptive, deceptive | | **Relative Grounds of Refusal** | Section 11 | Conflict with prior marks | | **Registration Validity Period** | **10 Years** | Section 25(1) | | **Renewal Period** | Every **10 Years** | Section 25(2) | | **Application Filing Authority** | Registrar of Trademarks | Section 3 | | **Symbols** | ™, ℠, ® | Unregistered vs. Registered | | **International Treaty** | Madrid Protocol (India acceded in **2013**) | Section 36A–36G | | **Infringement Provision** | Section 29 | Civil and Criminal remedies | | **Remedies Available** | Injunction, Damages, Account of Profits, Delivery-up | Sections 134–135 | | **Time Limit for Opposition** | **4 Months** (extendable by 2 months) | Section 21 | | **Well-Known Mark** | Recognized as a distinct category since **2017** | Trade Marks Rules, 2017 | ### 2.4 Functions and Real-World Utility of Trademarks 1. **Source Identification Function:** Distinguishes the origin of goods/services — central to consumer decision-making. 2. **Quality Assurance Function:** Signals consistent quality standards associated with the brand. 3. **Advertising Function:** Acts as a silent salesperson; a strong trademark reduces marketing costs over time. 4. **Economic Asset Function:** Trademarks appear on the **balance sheet** as intangible assets and contribute to brand valuation (e.g., Apple's brand value is in hundreds of billions of dollars). 5. **Legal Protection Function:** Grants the owner exclusive rights to use, license, and assign the mark. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Entrepreneurship Context:** For engineering startups (a focus of KTU's UCEST206 course), a trademark is often the **first form of IP** that a founder files. It protects the startup's brand name, logo, and product identifiers in the marketplace, building long-term competitive advantage. ## 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note for Students:** Since Trademarks is a legal-procedural topic (not a numerical engineering subject), the "derivation" here is a **step-by-step procedural breakdown** of the registration process, infringement analysis, and rights determination. KTU examiners reward structured, sequential answers in legal topics. ### 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedure for Trademark Registration in India **Step 1: Trademark Search** Conduct a search in the **IPO (Intellectual Property India) online database** to ensure the proposed mark is not identical or similar to an existing registered mark. This step is critical to avoid rejection at later stages. **Step 2: Filing of Application (Form TM-A)** Submit the application to the **Registrar of Trademarks** at the appropriate office (Mumbai, Delhi, Kolkata, Chennai, or Ahmedabad). The application must include: - Applicant's name and address - Representation of the mark - List of goods/services (classified under the **Nice Classification** of **45 classes**) - Date of first use (if any) - Power of Attorney (if filed through an agent) **Step 3: Examination by the Registrar** The examiner reviews the application against the absolute grounds (Section 9) and relative grounds (Section 11). The examiner issues an **Examination Report** within **3 months** (as per the Trade Marks Rules, 2017) listing objections, if any. **Step 4: Response to Examination Report** The applicant must respond to the objections within **30 days** (extendable by a further period not exceeding **3 months**). The response must address each objection with legal and factual arguments. **Step 5: Publication in the Trademark Journal** If accepted, the mark is **published in the Trademark Journal** to invite public opposition. The opposition window is **4 months** from the date of publication (extendable by 2 months, as per Rule 47). **Step 6: Opposition Proceedings (if any)** Any aggrieved party may file a notice of opposition within the stipulated period. Both parties are heard, and the Registrar passes a reasoned order. **Step 7: Registration and Certificate** If no opposition is filed, or if opposition is decided in favour of the applicant, the mark is **registered**, and a **Registration Certificate** is issued. **Step 8: Renewal** The registration is valid for **10 years** from the date of filing (Section 25(1)). It can be renewed indefinitely for successive periods of **10 years** upon payment of the prescribed fee. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Determining Trademark Infringement The following **decision matrix** is a high-yield framework for answering KTU questions on infringement: $$ \text{Infringement} = \begin{cases} \text{Yes, if} & \text{Mark is identical/similar AND} \\ & \text{Goods/Services are identical/similar AND} \\ & \text{Likelihood of confusion exists} \\ \text{No, if} & \text{Marks are sufficiently different OR} \\ & \text{Goods/Services are unrelated OR} \\ & \text{No likelihood of confusion } \end{cases} $$ **Step-by-Step Analysis Logic:** **Step 1:** Identify the **plaintiff's registered mark** and the **defendant's allegedly infringing mark**. **Step 2:** Compare the **visual similarity** (look), **phonetic similarity** (sound), and **conceptual similarity** (meaning). This is known as the **Triple Test** in trademark law. **Step 3:** Determine whether the **goods or services** are identical, similar, or unrelated under the Nice Classification. **Step 4:** Assess the **likelihood of confusion** from the perspective of an **average consumer** of ordinary intelligence, imperfect recollection, and without any specialized knowledge. **Step 5:** Apply the **relevant factors** as enumerated by the Hon'ble Supreme Court of India in the landmark case of *Cadila Health Care Ltd. v. Cadila Pharmaceuticals Ltd.* (2001) 5 SCC 73: - Nature of the marks - Degree of resemblance - Nature of the goods - Likely purchaser class - Mode of purchasing - Surrounding circumstances > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Tip for Marks:** While the marks "POLY-FLEX" (engineering polymer) and "POLI-FLEX" (textile) may appear similar, if the goods classes are different (Class 17 vs. Class 22) and the purchaser classes are different, infringement may not be made out. This nuance is what examiners test. ### 3.3 Rights Conferred by Trademark Registration (Section 28) A registered trademark owner has the **exclusive right** to use the mark in relation to the goods or services for which it is registered, and to obtain relief in respect of infringement. Specifically: 1. **Right to Use:** Exclusive use in commerce. 2. **Right to Assign:** Transfer ownership to another party. 3. **Right to License:** Grant permission to others (with or without conditions). 4. **Right to Sue:** File a suit for infringement (Section 134). 5. **Right to Seek Damages:** Claim monetary compensation for losses. 6. **Right to Injunction:** Obtain a court order to stop the infringer. ### 3.4 Defences Available Against Infringement (Section 30) The following are statutory defences a defendant can raise: - **Honest Concurrent Use:** The defendant has been using the mark honestly and concurrently. - **Prior Use:** The defendant was using the mark before the plaintiff's registration. - **Descriptive Use:** Use of the mark in a descriptive sense, not as a trademark. - **Own Name:** Use of one's own name or the name of one's place of business in the ordinary course of trade. - **Acquiescence:** The plaintiff has acquiesced in the use for a continuous period of **5 years**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Trademark Registration Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Brand Idea] --> B[Trademark Search
in IPO Database] B --> C{Search Result} C -->|Available| D[File Application
Form TM-A] C -->|Conflicting Mark| A D --> E[Examination by
Registrar] E --> F{Examination
Report} F -->|Objections Raised| G[File Response
within 30 Days] G --> H{Response
Accepted} F -->|No Objections| I[Publication in
Trademark Journal] H -->|No| J[Show Cause Hearing] J -->|Rejected| A H -->|Yes| I I --> K{Opposition
Filed} K -->|Yes, within 4 Months| L[Opposition
Hearing] L --> M{Decision} K -->|No Opposition| N[Registration Granted
Validity 10 Years] M -->|In Favour of Applicant| N M -->|Against Applicant| A N --> O[Renewal Every
10 Years] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Trademark Infringement Test ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Infringement Alleged] --> B[Step 1 Compare Marks] B --> C[Visual Similarity] B --> D[Phonetic Similarity] B --> E[Conceptual Similarity] C --> F{Likely Confusion?} D --> F E --> F F -->|Yes| G[Step 2 Compare Goods Services] F -->|No| H[No Infringement] G --> I{Same or Related Class?} I -->|Yes| J[Infringement Established] I -->|No| K[Assess Overall Likelihood
of Confusion] K -->|Confusion Likely| J K -->|No Confusion| H J --> L[Remedies Injunction Damages
Account of Profits Delivery Up] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Classification of Trademarks ```mermaid graph TB TM[Trademark] --> NAT[Based on Nature] TM --> FUN[Based on Function] NAT --> NAT1[Word Mark] NAT --> NAT2[Device Mark] NAT --> NAT3[Composite Mark] NAT --> NAT4[Sound Mark] NAT --> NAT5[Shape Mark] NAT --> NAT6[Colour Mark] FUN --> FUN1[Trademark for Goods] FUN --> FUN2[Service Mark] FUN --> FUN3[Certification Mark] FUN --> FUN4[Collective Mark] FUN --> FUN5[Well Known Mark] ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Rights of a Trademark Owner ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Registered Trademark Owner] --> B[Exclusive Right to Use] A --> C[Right to Assign] A --> D[Right to License] A --> E[Right to Sue for Infringement] A --> F[Right to Seek Damages] A --> G[Right to Obtain Injunction] A --> H[Right to Criminal Prosecution
under Section 103] B --> B1[Use in Course of Trade] C --> C1[Transfer Ownership via Deed] D --> D1[License with Quality Control] E --> E1[Civil Suit Section 134] F --> F1[Damages or Account of Profits] G --> G1[Temporary or Permanent Injunction] H --> H1[Imprisonment up to 3 Years
Fine up to 2 Lakh Rupees] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Define a trademark. List any four essential features of a valid trademark.** **Model Answer:** A trademark is a distinctive sign or symbol, represented graphically, that identifies and distinguishes the goods or services of one enterprise from those of another, as defined under Section 2(1)(zb) of the Trade Marks Act, 1999. **Four essential features of a valid trademark:** 1. **Distinctiveness:** It must be capable of distinguishing the goods or services of one trader from those of another. 2. **Graphical Representability:** The mark must be capable of being represented on paper (or electronically under the 2017 Rules). 3. **Non-deceptiveness:** The mark must not deceive or cause confusion among consumers regarding the nature, quality, or origin of the goods. 4. **Use in Commerce:** The mark must be used or proposed to be used in the course of trade. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]** **Distinguish between ™ and ® symbols.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | ™ (Trademark Symbol) | ® (Registered Symbol) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Nature** | Claim of ownership of an **unregistered** mark | Indicates a **registered** trademark | | **Legal Status** | No statutory recognition; merely a notice of claim | Legally protected under the Trade Marks Act, 1999 | | **Usage Rights** | Common law right based on use; weaker protection | Statutory right; stronger, nationwide protection | | **Penalty for Misuse** | None under statute | Misuse is a punishable offence under Section 107 | The ™ symbol may be used freely as soon as a person adopts a mark, while the ® symbol may be used **only after** the trademark is officially registered. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions in Module 1 carry 14 marks with internal choice. Two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Below are two alternative question choices. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]** **(a) "Trademarks serve multiple economic, legal, and commercial functions." Discuss the various functions of trademarks in the modern business environment. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** Trademarks perform several critical functions in the modern business environment: **1. Source Identification Function:** A trademark identifies the origin of goods or services. When a consumer sees a specific mark, they immediately associate it with a particular manufacturer. For instance, the "swoosh" instantly identifies Nike as the source of the sportswear. **2. Quality Assurance Function:** Trademarks assure consumers of a consistent level of quality. Once a consumer is satisfied with the quality of a product, the trademark serves as a guarantee that subsequent purchases will maintain the same standard. **3. Advertising and Brand Promotion Function:** A well-known trademark acts as a powerful advertising tool. Brands like Coca-Cola and Apple spend billions on marketing, with the trademark being the central element. A strong trademark reduces future marketing costs because the brand recall is already established. **4. Economic and Asset Function:** Trademarks are intangible assets that appear on a company's balance sheet. They contribute to brand valuation; for example, Apple Inc.'s brand value is consistently ranked among the top in the world. Trademarks can be sold, licensed, franchised, or used as collateral. **5. Legal Protection Function:** A registered trademark grants the owner exclusive legal rights to use, license, and assign the mark. It enables the owner to take legal action against infringers under Section 29 of the Trade Marks Act, 1999. **6. Consumer Protection Function:** By preventing consumer confusion, trademarks protect consumers from being misled into purchasing inferior or counterfeit goods. **Conclusion:** Trademarks are foundational to modern commerce, serving as legal instruments, economic assets, and communication tools that benefit both businesses and consumers. --- **(b) Explain the procedure for registration of a trademark in India under the Trade Marks Act, 1999. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The procedure for registration of a trademark in India is as follows: **Step 1: Trademark Search:** A search is conducted in the IPO online database to check for identical or similar existing marks. **Step 2: Filing of Application:** The application is filed in **Form TM-A** with the Registrar of Trademarks along with the prescribed fee, representation of the mark, and details of goods/services under the Nice Classification. **Step 3: Examination:** The Registrar examines the application under Sections 9 and 11. An Examination Report is issued within 3 months. **Step 4: Response to Objections:** The applicant must respond to any objections within 30 days (extendable to 3 months). **Step 5: Publication in the Trademark Journal:** Upon acceptance, the mark is published in the Journal for public opposition. **Step 6: Opposition Period:** Third parties may file opposition within 4 months (extendable by 2 months). **Step 7: Registration:** If no opposition is filed or it is decided in favour of the applicant, the mark is registered, and a certificate is issued. **Step 8: Renewal:** The registration is valid for **10 years** and can be renewed indefinitely for successive 10-year periods. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]** **(a) What is trademark infringement? Discuss the remedies available to the registered proprietor under the Trade Marks Act, 1999. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Trademark Infringement** refers to the unauthorized use of a registered trademark or a deceptively similar mark in relation to identical or similar goods or services, causing likelihood of confusion. It is governed by **Section 29** of the Trade Marks Act, 1999. **Types of Infringement:** 1. **Direct Infringement:** Use of an identical mark for identical goods/services. 2. **Indirect Infringement:** Use of a deceptively similar mark causing confusion. 3. **Infringement of Well-Known Marks:** Use of a well-known mark on unrelated goods that dilutes the distinctiveness of the mark. **Remedies Available to the Registered Proprietor:** **1. Civil Remedies (Section 134):** - **Injunction:** Temporary or permanent court order restraining the infringer. - **Damages or Account of Profits:** Monetary compensation for losses suffered. - **Delivery-Up:** Court order requiring the infringer to hand over infringing goods. **2. Criminal Remedies (Section 103):** - Imprisonment up to **3 years**, or - Fine up to **₹2 lakh**, or - Both, for selling or applying false trademarks. **3. Administrative Remedies:** - Seizure and destruction of counterfeit goods by Customs authorities at the border. - Anti-counterfeiting actions under the Customs Act, 1962. **4. Equitable Remedies:** - Anton Piller Order (search order for infringing goods). - Mareva Injunction (freezing the defendant's assets). --- **(b) Explain the various types of trademarks with suitable examples. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** Trademarks can be classified into the following types: **1. Word Mark:** A trademark consisting of words, letters, or numerals. Example: **Google**, **Kerala**, **IBM**. **2. Device/Logo Mark:** A graphical or pictorial design. Example: The **Apple** silhouette, the **Twitter** bird. **3. Composite Mark:** A combination of word and logo. Example: **Adidas** wordmark with three stripes, **Amazon** logo with the arrow. **4. Sound Mark:** A distinctive sound associated with a brand. Example: The **Nokia** ringtone, the **Intel** jingle, the **MGM** lion's roar. **5. Shape Mark:** A three-dimensional shape of a product or packaging. Example: The **Coca-Cola** bottle, the **Toblerone** triangular shape. **6. Colour Mark:** A single colour or combination used as a trademark. Example: **Cadbury's** purple, **Tiffany's** blue box. **7. Service Mark:** Used for services rather than goods. Example: The logo of **Jet Airways**, **HDFC Bank**. **8. Certification Mark:** Certifies the quality or origin of goods. Example: The **ISI** mark, the **AGMARK**, the **Woolmark**. **9. Collective Mark:** Used by a group of traders. Example: Marks of co-operative societies, trade associations. **10. Well-Known Mark:** A mark that has acquired widespread recognition. Example: **Coca-Cola**, **Pepsi**, **Microsoft**. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:** > > 1. **Confusing ™ with ®:** Many students incorrectly state that ™ indicates a registered trademark. The ® symbol alone denotes registration. Use ™ for unregistered marks and ® for registered marks. > 2. **Wrong Validity Period:** Some students write "15 years" — the correct period is **10 years**, renewable for successive 10-year periods. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this error. > 3. **Missing the Statutory Reference:** Always cite the **section number** of the Trade Marks Act, 1999. For example, "Section 2(1)(zb) for definition" or "Section 29 for infringement." Answers without statutory backing are treated as incomplete and lose 1-2 marks. > 4. **Ignoring International Framework:** KTU examiners reward students who mention the **Madrid Protocol (2013)** and **Nice Classification (45 classes)**. Failing to mention these shows incomplete knowledge. > 5. **Confusing Trademarks with Patents or Copyrights:** Trademarks protect **branding and source identification** (10 years, renewable). Patents protect **inventions** (20 years, non-renewable). Copyrights protect **creative works** (60+ years). Mixing these up is a common error. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Trademarks:** - **Governing Law:** Trade Marks Act, **1999** (India). - **Definition Section:** Section 2(1)(zb). - **Registration Period:** **10 years** from the date of filing. - **Renewal:** Every **10 years** indefinitely. - **Symbols:** ™ (unregistered), ℠ (unregistered service), ® (registered). - **Trademark Office Locations:** Mumbai, Delhi, Kolkata, Chennai, Ahmedabad. - **Classification System:** Nice Classification with **45 classes** (1–34 for goods, 35–45 for services). - **Absolute Grounds (Section 9):** Distinctiveness, descriptiveness, morality, public order, state emblems. - **Relative Grounds (Section 11):** Conflict with prior identical/similar marks. - **Opposition Window:** **4 months** from publication (extendable by 2 months). - **Infringement:** Section 29 — likelihood of confusion test (visual + phonetic + conceptual similarity). - **Civil Remedies:** Section 134 — Injunction, Damages, Account of Profits, Delivery-up. - **Criminal Penalties:** Section 103 — Imprisonment up to 3 years, fine up to ₹2 lakh. - **Well-Known Marks:** Recognized as a category in **2017** under the Trade Marks Rules, 2017. - **Madrid Protocol:** India acceded in **2013**, enabling international registration. - **Triple Test for Similarity:** Visual + Phonetic + Conceptual. - **Famous Case Law:** *Cadila Health Care Ltd. v. Cadila Pharmaceuticals Ltd.* (2001) 5 SCC 73. - **Entrepreneurship Tip:** A trademark is often the **first IP asset** a startup registers to build brand identity and long-term competitive advantage. --- **End of Notes — Trademarks | Module 1 | UCEST206 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Copyrights
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** UCEST206 - ENGINEERING ENTREPRENEURSHIP AND IPR
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Copyrights

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Copyright** is a statutory, exclusive legal right granted to the creator of original literary, dramatic, musical, and artistic works, to reproduce, publish, communicate, perform, and adapt the work for a specified period, in order to reward and incentivize creative authorship.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Anchor (Indian Context):** Section 13 of the **Copyright Act, 1957** (as amended by the Copyright (Amendment) Act 2012) defines the scope of copyrightable works in India. The Indian statute is compliant with the **Berne Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works (1886)** and the **TRIPS Agreement (1994)**.

The expression "**copyright**" itself is a contraction of the right to **copy**. It is a **bundle of exclusive rights** that the law automatically vests in the author the moment an original work is fixed in a tangible medium of expression — *no formal registration is mandatory in India* (though registration confers evidentiary advantages).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Invisible Fence Around Your Idea" Analogy**
> Imagine you spend three months hand-painting a beautiful mural on your apartment wall. The moment the paint dries, you do not need to file any government paperwork for people to *acknowledge* that it is yours. Copyright is the **invisible, legal fence** that automatically surrounds your mural. It tells the rest of the world: *"You may look, appreciate, and even quote — but you cannot photograph it, sell prints of it, or paint a copy on their wall without my permission."* The fence is invisible, automatic, and time-bound (it expires), and breaking it triggers legal penalties — much like a court fine for trespassing.

For an **engineering student**, the most relevant real-world application is understanding that **the source code you write, the technical drawings you produce, the project thesis you submit, and even the PCB layout you design** are *automatically* protected by copyright the moment they are saved. Copying a classmate's project report, code, or CAD drawing without attribution is therefore a **copyright violation**, not merely a moral shortcoming.

## 1.3 What Copyright Protects — and What It Does Not

| **Protected (Expression)** | **NOT Protected (Idea / Fact / System)** |
| :--- | :--- |
| The exact text of a novel | The underlying plot idea |
| Specific lines of source code | The algorithm it implements |
| A recorded song waveform | The chord progression in isolation |
| A photograph of a monument | The monument itself (if public) |
| A technical drawing's lines | The engineering principle it depicts |

> [!WARNING]
> **The Idea–Expression Dichotomy (Apex KTU Concept):** Copyright protects the **expression of ideas**, never the **idea itself**. This is the single most-tested distinction in KTU examinations on this topic. An idea to build a foldable phone is free; Apple's specific industrial drawings of the iPhone Fold are protected.

## 1.4 Key Terminology — Standard KTU Glossary

- **Author:** The natural person who created the work (Section 2(d) of the Act).
- **Work of Joint Authorship:** A work produced by two or more authors in which their contributions are inseparable (Section 2(z)).
- **Infringement:** Any act done by a third party that contravenes the exclusive rights of the copyright owner (Section 51).
- **Fair Dealing:** Statutory exception allowing limited use for research, private study, criticism, review, or news reporting (Section 52).
- **Collective Administration:** Rights managed by societies (e.g., IPRS, IRRO) on behalf of authors.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Pillar Foundation of Copyright Law

Copyright doctrine, in every major jurisdiction (Berne Convention members), rests on three conceptual pillars. KTU examiners frequently frame Part A (3-mark) questions around these.

### Pillar 1 — Originality
The work must originate from the author and must involve a *modicum of creativity*. In India, the Supreme Court in *Eastern Book Company v. D.B. Modak (2008)* held that a *judicious arrangement* of headnotes constitutes original literary work. For engineering, **original code logic, unique schematic layouts, and creative UI designs** easily satisfy this threshold.

### Pillar 2 — Fixation in a Tangible Medium
The work must be recorded in some physical or digital form (paper, hard drive, server, canvas). Purely **unrecorded mental choreographies or unstated algorithms are not protected** until fixed.

### Pillar 3 — Categorization under Section 13
The work must fall within one of the eight categories enumerated in the Act (see Concept Sheet below).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table. It is the most frequently asked framework in KTU Module 1 questions.

| **S.No.** | **Category of Work (Sec. 13)** | **Examples Relevant to Engineers** | **Term of Copyright** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Literary works** (Sec. 2(o)) | Project reports, software code, technical manuals, databases | Life of author $\vert$ 60 years |
| 2 | **Dramatic works** | Screenplays for instructional videos, stage demos | Life of author $\vert$ 60 years |
| 3 | **Musical works** | Soundtracks for presentations, podcast intros | Life of author $\vert$ 60 years |
| 4 | **Artistic works** | PCB layouts, CAD drawings, engineering diagrams, logos | Life of author $\vert$ 60 years |
| 5 | **Cinematograph films** | Recorded lab demonstrations, MOOC videos | 60 years from publication |
| 6 | **Sound recordings** | Lecture audio, audio project demos | 60 years from publication |
| 7 | **Broadcasts** | University radio/webcast sessions | 25 years from broadcast |
| 8 | **Government / Public Sector works** | B.Tech syllabus documents, PSUs' reports | 60 years from publication |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Isolation Rule:** Throughout this module, the notation $\vert$ is used in the "Term of Copyright" column to mean *plus* (e.g., Life of author $\vert$ 60 years = "Lifetime + 60 years"). It is **not** an absolute value operator and must not be confused with mathematical magnitude.

## 2.3 Rights Conferred by Copyright (Section 14)

The copyright owner holds **two distinct classes of rights**:

### A. Economic Rights (Commercial / Property Rights)
These can be assigned or licensed to others for monetary consideration:
1. **Right to Reproduce** — copy the work in any form.
2. **Right to Publish** — issue copies to the public.
3. **Right to Communicate** — broadcast, stream, or transmit (critical for e-learning).
4. **Right to Adapt / Translate** — convert the work into another form.
5. **Right to Perform** — in the case of dramatic/musical works.
6. **Right to Sell / Rent** — distribute copies commercially.

### B. Moral Rights (Section 57)
These are **inalienable** and remain with the author even after assignment of economic rights:
1. **Right of Paternity (Attribution):** The right to claim authorship and to prevent false attribution.
2. **Right of Integrity:** The right to prevent distortion, mutilation, or modification that harms the author's honour or reputation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Indian copyright law, unlike US law, expressly recognizes moral rights and they are **perpetual** (no time limit), and they pass to the legal heirs upon the author's death. This is a guaranteed short-note question in Part A.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Open-Source Software Compliance:** Understanding copyright is essential for complying with licenses like **GPL, MIT, Apache** — the B.Tech final-year project often depends on open-source components, and violating their copyright terms leads to legal liability.
- **Plagiarism Detection in Academia:** Universities use software (Turnitin) precisely because copying text/code triggers copyright (and academic integrity) issues.
- **Patent vs. Copyright Overlap:** While patent protects the *idea* of an invention, copyright protects the *document* describing the invention. Engineers must grasp both to file complete IP protection.
- **Digital Content Creation:** Engineers who create YouTube tutorials, blogs, or MOOCs must license music, images, and code under permissible terms.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Legal Reasoning Chain

## 3.1 The "Is It Copyright?" Analytical Matrix

A KTU Part B (14-mark) question often presents a *scenario* and asks whether copyright subsists, who owns it, and whether infringement occurred. Use this **5-step deductive framework** in your answers:

### Step 1 — Identify the Work
Determine the exact subject matter. Is it a literary work, artistic work, cinematograph film, etc., as enumerated in Section 13?

### Step 2 — Test for Originality
Ask: Did the work originate from the author with at least a *modicum of creativity*? Mere trivial effort (e.g., typing the periodic table) is excluded by the *Sweat of the Brow* exception debate — India moved past this in *Eastern Book Co. v. Modak*.

### Step 3 — Test for Fixation
Is the work recorded in a tangible medium? Lecture notes saved on a hard disk = fixed. Unrecorded verbal explanation = not protected.

### Step 4 — Identify the Author / First Owner
Apply **Section 17** of the Act. Default rule: the author is the first owner. Exceptions:
- Work created during employment under a contract of service → **employer owns it** (e.g., code written by a full-time R&D engineer).
- Work commissioned for valuable consideration (photographs, portraits, engravings) → **commissioner owns it** unless contract says otherwise.
- Work created by a Government employee in official capacity → **Government owns it**.

### Step 5 — Apply Infringement Test (Section 51)
Compare the alleged infringing work with the original. A work is infringed if a "substantial part" is copied — *not necessarily the whole*. Quality matters more than quantity. The test is whether an **ordinary, reasonable person** would conclude that copying occurred.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Analytical Walkthrough

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question):** *"Aryan, a B.Tech student at ABC Engineering College, Kerala, develops a unique MATLAB GUI for solar panel optimization as his final-year project. His classmate Bhaskar copies the entire code, modifies variable names, and submits it to a startup that commercializes the GUI without Aryan's consent. Discuss the copyright implications, including ownership, infringement, and remedies."*

### Solution Framework (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Identify the Work:**
The MATLAB GUI source code qualifies as a **literary work** under Section 2(o) of the Copyright Act, 1957, as software is expressly included in the definition of "literary work" per the 2012 amendment and the WIPO Copyright Treaty (1996).

**Step 2 — Test for Originality:**
Aryan's original code logic, GUI layout, and unique optimization algorithm satisfy the originality threshold. The *modicum of creativity* standard is easily met.

**Step 3 — Test for Fixation:**
The code is stored on Aryan's laptop and project repository — fixation is satisfied.

**Step 4 — Identify the First Owner:**
Apply Section 17. Since Aryan developed the project in his capacity as a *student* (not under a formal contract of service with the college, unless the project guide's institution has a specific IP policy stating otherwise), **Aryan is the first owner** of the copyright in his code.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Sub-Step — College IP Policy:** Many KTU-affiliated colleges have Institutional IP Policies that may assign project-related IP to the college. The exam answer should acknowledge this caveat: *in the absence of a contrary agreement, Aryan is presumed to be the first owner.*

**Step 5 — Infringement Analysis:**

$$\text{Infringement Established If: } (\text{Substantial Similarity}) \land (\text{Access to Original}) \land (\text{No Statutory Defence})$$

$$\text{Bhaskar's Action} = \text{Reproduction (Sec. 51(a)(i))} + \text{Violation of Sec. 14 Rights}$$

Bhaskar copied the *substantial part* of the code. Renaming variables is a **colourable imitation** — a legal doctrine where someone attempts to evade infringement by making cosmetic changes. Indian courts (e.g., *R.G. Anand v. M/s Delux Films*, 1978) hold that **colourable imitation constitutes infringement**.

**Step 6 — Remedies Available to Aryan:**

| **Civil Remedies (Sec. 55–62)** | **Criminal Remedies (Sec. 63–70)** | **Administrative Remedies** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Injunctions (temporary/permanent) | Imprisonment up to **3 years** | Seizure of infringing copies |
| Damages or account of profits | Fine up to **₹2 lakh** (extendable) | Police raid under Sec. 64 |
| Delivery of infringing copies | For second offence: enhanced penalty | Customs border seizure |

## 3.3 Fair Dealing Defence (Section 52)

Bhaskar could *potentially* defend by claiming **fair dealing** for private study or research. However, the burden of proof lies on the defendant, and the Supreme Court test (*Hyderabad Industries v. S. Sanyasi Rao*, 1995) requires:
- **Purpose** must be non-commercial.
- **Quantum** must be reasonable and necessary.
- **Effect** must not compete with the original.

Bhaskar's commercial submission to a startup **fails all three limbs**, so the defence is unavailable.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Copyright Framework — Block-Level Functional Architecture
